narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 60.7% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This threshold is set to prioritize admitting those likely to succeed, and unfortunately, your profile tipped the scales toward that riskier outcome. You have solid aspects, like an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 that's right in line with the typical applicant's 3.21, an upper-middle family income background, and being a full-time student—traits shared by most in the dataset. You're also a white female, which aligns closely with the majority (over 80% white and nearly half female), so those didn't stand out unusually. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the average of 5.53—was the single most influential factor, strongly signaling to the model a higher risk of bar failure compared to peers who ranked higher. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 31, well under the typical 36.7, which pushed the prediction further toward failure as lower scores often correlate with bar struggles in similar students. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also much lower than the average 5.71, came next in influence, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance. Family income level followed as moderately influential in the same direction, then your undergraduate GPA edged the prediction slightly toward risk despite being average. Your racial background had a smaller push toward the failure prediction, while being female offered a tiny counterbalance toward success, and full-time status was negligible. Overall, these academic markers—especially your low Year 3 and Year 1 deciles and LSAT—dominated the decision, setting you apart from typical students who pass the bar after stronger showings there. To boost your future odds, focus on realistic steps like intensive LSAT retakes or test prep to build exam skills, as that was a top predictor here; seek academic support to lift your performance in advanced coursework, mirroring your decile gaps; and consider targeted bar exam study programs proven to help students with similar profiles succeed on the first try. With targeted effort on these, you could shift your profile toward the successful majority. Hang in there—many overcome these hurdles with persistence.
